learning_outcomes.description: Apply a broad understanding of historical, geographical, cultural and socio-political contexts to Australian and Pacific Indigenous Arts
Develop cross-cultural and global awareness that upholds indigenous cultural protocols and perspectives
Articulate key ideas and themes about Australia Pacific indigenous arts in written and oral formats
Conduct useful, relevant and innovative research in critical discourse about Australia Pacific indigenous arts for the discipline of art history and the arts industry
Develop skills in exhibition literacy and curatorial development

assessments.task_description:
Description:

A weekly written critical reflection via the course Blackboard discussion board. Students are required to respond to a question or provocation that will be posted each week (Weeks 4-10) relating to course content, including lecture and tutorial material and/or readings. Responses should demonstrate genuine personal engagement with and specific reference to course content, alongside critical reflection on the ideas, perspectives, and debates encountered each week. Students are required to write in the first person and respond individually and directly to the question, however engagement with the contributions of their peers is also welcomed and can inform their response.

There will be seven (7) questions/provocations in total, corresponding with teaching weeks 4-10. Students will be marked on the best five (5) responses out of seven (7). Each of the five (5) best responses will be worth four (4) marks each, totaling twenty (20) marks for the critical reflection assessment.

Questions/provocations will be posted at the end of the weekly lecture, and responses will be due for submission by the beginning of the following week’s lecture, giving students one week to complete each task.

Note the Week 9 task will be due in the first week back after the mid-semester break in Week 10.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Please use the discussion link on the course Blackboard to submit your weekly responses.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Description:

A 2000-word research essay on one (1) of the following questions. This assessment demonstrates research skills, critical analysis, and scholarly writing in the context of Australian and Pacific Indigenous art histories.

Students must write one (1) research essay of 2000 words on any one (1) of the following questions:

1.        Discuss the ethical responsibilities of artists, researchers, and institutions when engaging with Indigenous cultures and knowledge systems through art. Consider oral histories, land-based knowledges, and worldviews of Indigenous arts and use examples to illustrate how these carry meanings between artists, cultures, and audiences. Reflect on the balance between innovation, protocols, cultural authorities, and shared access especially in contemporary or globalised art contexts.

2.        Examine how cultural narrative forms are reimagined in contemporary Indigenous art to address present-day struggles, identities, climate or land justice. Consider the different ways Indigenous artists assert presence, modes of truth, and cultural survival through art. How does visual language critique, protest, or heal in response to colonial and institutional systems. Reflect on the power dynamics of the (in)visible, heard, or archived.

3.        Discuss ways Indigenous art functions as a sovereign archive of knowledge that resists, defines, interprets, and transmits Indigenous cultures and art histories outside of museum or colonial frameworks. Focus on how sovereignty is expressed not just through politics, but through visual, spiritual, and ancestral continuities in art. Reflect on the disruptions Indigenous art presents to linear timelines or colonials agendas. Consider how motifs, methods, and meanings are historical, embodied, and evolving.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Description:

This exam task asks you to apply the curatorial and art historical knowledge gained across the course to develop a short exhibition proposal. During the exam, you will be provided with twenty (20) images drawn from the artworks covered in Weeks 4–6 of the course (both lectures and tutorials). From these images, you will select eight (8) of them to form the basis of your proposed exhibition, and write in depth about four (4) of them, in relation to their inclusion in the proposed exhibition.

While images will be drawn from the artworks covered in Weeks 4–6, your curatorial theme and rationale may draw on ideas, contexts, and arguments from across the whole course.

Your proposal should demonstrate your understanding of Australian and Pacific Indigenous art histories, cultural contexts, and perspectives, your ability to develop a coherent and informed curatorial argument, your capacity to think critically about how artworks speak to each other and to audiences, and your consideration of how an exhibition is presented and situated in space.

As part of the exam, students will be required to write to the following.

·      Exhibition title

·      Curatorial theme, rationale, and significance (approx. 400 words) — clearly introduce and outline your exhibition curatorial theme, explain why it is significant in the context of Australian and Pacific Indigenous art histories and socio-political and cultural contexts, and briefly identify the intended audience for the exhibition. Your curatorial argument should demonstrate informed and respectful engagement with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander cultural contexts and perspectives relevant to your selected works and theme.

·      List of eight (8) selected works—select eight (8) of the twenty (20) works provided to be included in the exhibition. Each of the 20 images provided will include the artist’s name, artwork title, date, and medium.

·      Justification of four (4) selected works (400 words, approximately 100 words per work) — for each of your four (4) works, consider the formal and/or contextual analysis of the work and/or artist, and contextualise the work in relation to your curatorial theme, explaining how it contributes to the overall rationale of the exhibition.

·      Spatial and venue considerations (approx. 200 words) — describe how you would present the exhibition in space, including the venue and its significance, the sequencing or grouping of works, and any relevant display or hanging considerations.

General Guidelines:

·      This is a secure assessment via online exam. Software and other access requirements will be shared via the course Blackboard.

Academic Integrity: Ensure adherence to UQ Academic Integrity and Student Conduct standards.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.
